title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: seroreconstruct R package Bayesian inference influenza virus infection status serial antibody measurements. relaxes traditional 4-fold rise rule jointly modeling antibody boosting infection, waning time, measurement error — estimating individual-level infection probabilities hemagglutination inhibition (HAI) titer data. statistical framework described Tsang et al. (2022) Nature Communications 13:1557.

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: package requires two data frames: age_group: integer (0 = children, 1 = adults, 2 = older adults) start_time, end_time: follow-period (integer days) time1, time2, time3: serum collection dates (integer days) HAI_titer_1, HAI_titer_2, HAI_titer3: HAI titers collection inputILI — daily influenza-like illness activity, rows corresponding consecutive days. row indices must cover range start_time end_time inputdata.

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: main function sero_reconstruct() runs Bayesian MCMC estimate infection probabilities antibody dynamics parameters. real analyses, use least 100,000 iterations appropriate burn-thinning. vignette, use short run illustration:
code: fit <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                         n_iteration = 200000, burnin = 100000, thinning = 10) fit <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                         n_iteration = 5000, burnin = 2000, thinning = 1) #> 1000 #> 2000 #> 3000 #> 4000 #> MCMC complete in 64 seconds. Use summary() to view estimates. fit #> seroreconstruct fit #>   Individuals: 1753  #>   Age groups: 3  #>   Posterior samples: 3000  #>   Runtime: 64 seconds #>  #> Use summary() to extract model estimates.

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: summary() method extracts key estimates 95% credible intervals: summary table includes: Random error (%) Two-fold error (%): measurement error parameters Boosting: fold-increase antibody titer infection Waning: fold-decrease antibody titer per year post-infection Infection probability: overall stratified baseline HAI titer
code: summary(fit) #>                                                                                                        Variable #>                                                                                                Random error (%) #>                                                                                              Two-fold error (%) #>                                                           Fold-increase after infection for children (Boosting) #>                                                                Fold-decrease after 1 year for children (Waning) #>                                                                Fold-increase after 1 year for adults (Boosting) #>                                                                  Fold-decrease after 1 year for adults (Waning) #>                                                                              Infection probability for children #>                                                                                Infection probability for adults #>                                                                          Infection probability for older adults #>                                             Infection probability for children with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10 #>                                               Infection probability for adults with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10 #>                                         Infection probability for older adults with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10 #>                                                                        Relative risk for children (Ref: Adults) #>                                                                    Relative risk for older adults (Ref: Adults) #>      Relative risk for children with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10 (Ref: Adults with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10) #>  Relative risk for older adults with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10 (Ref: Adults with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10) #>  Point estimate Lower bound Upper bound #>            1.56        0.97        2.45 #>            4.05        4.93        3.11 #>           11.47        7.28       16.16 #>            1.03        1.01        1.06 #>            7.46        5.95        9.21 #>            1.05        1.02        1.08 #>            0.17        0.14        0.21 #>            0.19        0.15        0.22 #>            0.13        0.09        0.18 #>            0.46        0.39        0.55 #>            0.30        0.25        0.36 #>            0.21        0.14        0.29 #>            0.95        0.72        1.21 #>            1.57        1.25        1.93 #>            0.71        0.47        0.99 #>            0.72        0.49        1.00

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: Check convergence trace plots posterior density plots:
code: plot_diagnostics(fit, params = c("random_error", "twofold_error"))

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: Visualize posterior trajectories individual participants. Red lines show trajectories infection occurred; blue lines show trajectories without infection.
code: plot_trajectory(fit, id = 1)

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
code: oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) plot_boosting(fit) plot_waning(fit) par(oldpar)

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: Forest plot posterior infection probabilities 95% credible intervals:
code: plot_infection_prob(fit)

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: Extract parameter estimates individual-level results data frames:

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: Compare infection rates across groups fitting independent MCMCs:
code: fit_by_age <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                                n_iteration = 200000, burnin = 100000,                                thinning = 10, group_by = ~age_group)  summary(fit_by_age)

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: comparing groups, measurement error lab assay property — shared across groups. can additionally share boosting/waning groups expected similar antibody dynamics:
code: # Share all parameters except infection probability fit_joint <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                               n_iteration = 200000, burnin = 100000,                               thinning = 10,                               group_by = ~age_group,                               shared = c("error", "boosting_waning"))  summary(fit_joint)

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: Generate synthetic data power analysis validation: simulated data can passed directly sero_reconstruct() simulation-recovery studies.
code: data("para1") data("para2")  sim <- simulate_data(inputdata, flu_activity, para1, para2) names(sim) #> [1] "age_group"   "start_time"  "end_time"    "time1"       "time2"       #> [6] "time3"       "HAI_titer_1" "HAI_titer_2" "HAI_titer3"

title: Getting Started with seroreconstruct
text: cite seroreconstruct publications: Tsang TK, Perera RAPM, Fang VJ, Wong JY, Shiu EY, HC, Ip DKM, Malik Peiris JS, Leung GM, Cowling BJ, Cauchemez S. (2022). Reconstructing antibody dynamics estimate risk influenza virus infection. Nature Communications 13:1557. doi: 10.1038/s41467-022-29310-8

title: Statistical Methodology
text: seroreconstruct implements Bayesian framework inferring influenza virus infection serial hemagglutination inhibition (HAI) antibody titers. model accounts : Antibody boosting infection Antibody waning time Measurement error HAI assay Heterogeneity baseline titers infection risk full statistical details described Tsang et al. (2022) Nature Communications 13:1557.

title: Statistical Methodology
text: individual ii baseline titer BiB_i (log2 scale), true antibody trajectory : infected (Zi=0Z_i = 0): Ỹ(t)=Bi⋅e−w⋅t/365\tilde{Y}_i(t) = B_i \cdot e^{-w \cdot t / 365} infected (Zi=1Z_i = 1 time TiT_i): Pre-infection: exponential decay baseline Infection: linear 14-day boosting ramp magnitude bb (log2 scale) Post-infection: exponential decay boosted level

title: Statistical Methodology
text: observed titer Yi,tY_{,t} related true titer Ỹ(t)\tilde{Y}_i(t) : Random error: Gaussian noise standard deviation σr\sigma_r Two-fold error: probability p2fp_{2f}, observed titer shifted one dilution step (HAI assay measures 2-fold dilutions: $<$10, 10, 20, 40, 80, …) parameters properties laboratory assay shared across individuals groups.

title: Statistical Methodology
text: probability infection individual ii age group aa depends influenza activity baseline HAI titer: P(Zi=1)=1−exp(−λa⋅∑tILI(t)⋅exp(β⋅Bi/10))P(Z_i = 1) = 1 - \exp\left(-\lambda_a \cdot \sum_t \text{ILI}(t) \cdot \exp(\beta \cdot B_i / 10)\right) ILI(t)\text{ILI}(t) daily influenza-like illness rate, λa\lambda_a age-specific infection hazard scaling, β\beta HAI titer protection coefficient (negative values indicate protective effect higher baseline titers).

title: Statistical Methodology
text: model uses weakly informative priors. MCMC uses Metropolis-Hastings updates adaptive proposal tuning burn-. Key features: Infection times TiT_i sampled uniformly within follow-period Baseline titers drawn empirical distribution observed pre-season titers Parameter proposals use truncated normal distributions respect natural constraints (e.g., boosting >0> 0, waning >0> 0)

title: Statistical Methodology
text: posterior sampled using custom C++ MCMC sampler (via Rcpp/RcppArmadillo) following structure: Gibbs updates discrete parameters (infection status, infection time) Metropolis-Hastings updates continuous parameters (boosting, waning, error, infection hazard, HAI coefficient) Parallel chains via RcppParallel (optional)

title: Statistical Methodology
text: fitting, check convergence using: Trace plots: plot_diagnostics(fit) — look stationarity (trends) good mixing (long excursions) Effective sample size: internal ESS calculation uses initial positive sequence estimator (Geyer, 1992) Multiple chains: fit model multiple times different random seeds compare posterior distributions

title: Statistical Methodology
text: posterior samples stored fit$posterior_model_parameter, matrix rows = posterior samples columns = parameters: Total: 7+G7 + G parameters per season. multi-season fits, infection hazard HAI coefficient replicated per season: 6+G×S+S6 + G \times S + S total parameters.

title: Statistical Methodology
text: comparing groups (e.g., vaccinated vs unvaccinated), measurement error always shared reflects laboratory assay, biology. Boosting waning can optionally shared group-specific: shared = c("error", "boosting_waning"): groups share error antibody dynamics; infection risk differs shared = "error": error shared group boosting waning rates

title: Statistical Methodology
text: Tsang TK, Perera RAPM, Fang VJ, Wong JY, Shiu EY, HC, Ip DKM, Malik Peiris JS, Leung GM, Cowling BJ, Cauchemez S. (2022). Reconstructing antibody dynamics estimate risk influenza virus infection. Nature Communications 13:1557. doi: 10.1038/s41467-022-29310-8 Geyer CJ. (1992). Practical Markov chain Monte Carlo. Statistical Science 7(4):473–483.

what: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
text: seroreconstruct Bayesian modeling framework infer influenza virus infection status, antibody dynamics, individual infection risks serological data, accounting measurement error. identify influenza infections relaxing 4-fold rise rule, quantifies contributions age pre-epidemic hemagglutination-inhibiting (HAI) titers infection risk.

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
text: Bayesian MCMC inference infection probability, antibody boosting/waning, measurement error Multi-season support — fit season-specific infection risk HAI protection parameters Subgroup comparisons via group_by — fit independent MCMCs age groups, vaccination status, strata Shared parameters via shared — run joint model shares measurement error /boosting/waning across groups estimating group-specific infection risk S3 classes print() summary() methods clean output Publication-ready plots — antibody trajectories, boosting/waning distributions, infection probability forest plots, MCMC diagnostics Summary tables — parameter estimates credible intervals, per-individual infection probabilities Subject ID tracking — pass subject_ids sero_reconstruct() ID-based individual lookup plots Simulation — generate synthetic datasets validation power analysis

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("timktsang/seroreconstruct")

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
code: library(seroreconstruct)  # Load example data data("inputdata") data("flu_activity")  # Fit the model (use more iterations for real analyses, e.g. 200000) fit <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                         n_iteration = 2000, burnin = 1000, thinning = 1)  # View results summary(fit)

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
text: Red lines show posterior trajectories infection; blue lines show trajectories without infection. Black dots observed HAI titers.
code: plot_trajectory(fit, id = 1)

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
text: Violin plots posterior fold-rise antibody titer infection, median crossbar 95% credible interval.
code: plot_boosting(fit)

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
text: Posterior median 95% credible band antibody remaining time since infection.
code: plot_waning(fit)

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
text: Forest plot posterior infection probabilities. Supports combining multiple fits section headers multi-group comparisons.
code: plot_infection_prob(fit,   labels = c("Children", "Adults", "Older adults"))

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
code: # Parameter estimates with credible intervals table_parameters(fit)  # Per-individual infection probabilities table_infections(fit)

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
text: Fit separate models age group:
code: fit_by_age <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                                n_iteration = 20000, burnin = 10000,                                thinning = 5, group_by = ~age_group)  # View combined results summary(fit_by_age)  # Access individual group fits summary(fit_by_age[["1"]])

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
text: comparing groups (e.g., vaccinated vs unvaccinated), parameters biologically shared (measurement error, antibody dynamics) infection risk differs groups. Use shared run single joint MCMC shares specified parameters: Available shared parameter types: Infection probability HAI protection always group-specific.
code: # Share measurement error and boosting/waning across vaccine groups fit_joint <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                               n_iteration = 20000, burnin = 10000,                               thinning = 5,                               group_by = ~vaccine,                               shared = c("error", "boosting_waning"))  print(fit_joint)

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
text: Add season column (0-indexed integer) input data:
code: # Stack data from multiple seasons inputdata$season <- 0L  # single season example  # For multi-season: combine data frames with season = 0, 1, 2, ... # The model estimates season-specific infection risk and HAI protection fit_multi <- sero_reconstruct(multi_season_data, flu_activity,                               n_iteration = 20000, burnin = 10000,                               thinning = 5)

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection
text: Generate synthetic data validation:
code: data("para1")  # example parameter vector (single season) data("para2")  # baseline HAI titer distribution  simulated <- simulate_data(inputdata, flu_activity, para1, para2)

title: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection

what: Example of parameter vector for the main model — para1
title: Example of parameter vector for the main model — para1
text: example parameter vector main model used seroreconstruct function. data frame specifies format parameter vector main model.

title: Example of parameter vector for the main model — para1
code: data(para1)

title: Example of parameter vector for the main model — para1
text: numeric vector 10 elements (single-season model, S = 1).   general length 6 + 4*S S number seasons. Elements 1–6 (shared) 1) random measurement error, 2) 2-fold error,     3) boosting children (log2), 4) waning children (log2),     5) boosting adults (log2), 6) waning adults (log2). Elements 7–9 (per-season) infection risk scale parameters     children, adults, older adults (3 per season). Element 10 (per-season) log risk ratio 2-fold increase     baseline HAI titer (1 per season).

what: Example of parameter vector for the baseline HAI titer for the main model — para2

code: data(para2)

title: Example of parameter vector for the baseline HAI titer for the main model — para2
text: numeric vector 20 elements (single-season model, S = 1).   general length 20 * S S number seasons. Elements 1–10 probability HAI titer 0–9 children Elements 11–20 probability HAI titer 0–9 adults multi-season models, pattern repeats season.

title: Plot posterior boosting distributions — plot_boosting

code: plot_boosting(fit, cols = NULL, main = NULL, show_legend = TRUE, ...)

title: Plot posterior boosting distributions — plot_boosting
text: fit seroreconstruct_fit seroreconstruct_joint object. single-season fits currently supported. cols Optional character vector colors, one per group. main Optional plot title. show_legend Logical; whether draw legend. Default TRUE. ... Additional graphical parameters passed plot().

title: Plot posterior boosting distributions — plot_boosting
text: Invisible NULL. Called side effect producing plot.

title: Plot posterior boosting distributions — plot_boosting
code: # \donttest{ fit <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                         n_iteration = 2000, burnin = 1000, thinning = 1) #> 1000 #> MCMC complete in 27 seconds. Use summary() to view estimates. plot_boosting(fit)  # }

title: MCMC diagnostic plots — plot_diagnostics
text: Produces trace plots posterior density plots model parameter. Trace plots show MCMC chain posterior mean (red dashed line). Density plots show marginal posterior 95% credible interval bounds (blue dashed lines).

title: MCMC diagnostic plots — plot_diagnostics
code: plot_diagnostics(fit, params = NULL)

title: MCMC diagnostic plots — plot_diagnostics
text: fit seroreconstruct_fit, seroreconstruct_joint, seroreconstruct_multi object. params Optional character vector parameter names plot. NULL (default), parameters plotted. Use table_parameters(fit)$Parameter see available names.

title: MCMC diagnostic plots — plot_diagnostics
text: Invisible NULL. Called side effect producing plots.

title: MCMC diagnostic plots — plot_diagnostics
code: # \donttest{ fit <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                         n_iteration = 2000, burnin = 1000, thinning = 1) #> 1000 #> MCMC complete in 28 seconds. Use summary() to view estimates. # Plot selected parameters (use params = NULL for all) plot_diagnostics(fit, params = c("random_error", "twofold_error"))  # }

title: Plot infection probabilities (forest plot) — plot_infection_prob
text: Forest plot showing posterior infection probabilities 95% credible intervals. Supports single fits, multi-group fits, combining results multiple fits section headers.

title: Plot infection probabilities (forest plot) — plot_infection_prob
code: plot_infection_prob(   fits,   labels = NULL,   main = NULL,   file = NULL,   width = 8,   height = NULL,   xlim = NULL,   cex = 0.85,   ... )

title: Plot infection probabilities (forest plot) — plot_infection_prob
text: fits seroreconstruct_fit, seroreconstruct_joint, seroreconstruct_multi object, named list fit objects. named list provided, names used section headers. labels Optional character vector custom labels strata within fit. named list fits, use list character vectors. main Optional plot title. file Optional file path PDF output. Default: NULL (current device). width PDF width inches. Default: 8. height PDF height inches. Default: auto-calculated. xlim Numeric vector length 2 x-axis range (probability scale, e.g. c(0, 0.5)). Default: auto-determined. cex Character expansion factor. Default: 0.85. ... Additional graphical parameters passed plot().

title: Plot infection probabilities (forest plot) — plot_infection_prob
text: Invisible data frame plotted estimates (Stratum, Probability,   Lower, Upper).

title: Plot infection probabilities (forest plot) — plot_infection_prob
code: # \donttest{ fit <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                         n_iteration = 2000, burnin = 1000, thinning = 1) #> 1000 #> MCMC complete in 27 seconds. Use summary() to view estimates. plot_infection_prob(fit)  # }

what: Plot antibody trajectory with model fit — plot_trajectory
title: Plot antibody trajectory with model fit — plot_trajectory
text: single individual, draws posterior-sampled antibody trajectories overlaid observed HAI titers. Red lines show trajectories infection occurred; blue lines show trajectories without infection. Matches visualization style Figure 1B Tsang et al. (2022).

title: Plot antibody trajectory with model fit — plot_trajectory
code: plot_trajectory(   fit,   id = 1,   subjects = NULL,   n_samples = 100,   main = NULL,   col_infected = NULL,   col_uninfected = NULL,   show_legend = TRUE,   ... )

title: Plot antibody trajectory with model fit — plot_trajectory
text: fit seroreconstruct_fit seroreconstruct_joint object. id Row index (integer) subject identifier plot. Numeric values valid row range (1 N) treated 1-based row indices. Numeric values outside range looked subject_ids subjects available. Non-numeric values always looked subject identifier. subjects Optional vector subject identifiers aligned fit rows. Use subject_ids provided fitting time. Example: subjects = inputdata$subject_id. n_samples Number posterior samples draw. Default 100. main Optional plot title. NULL, default title individual index posterior infection probability generated. col_infected Color infected trajectories. Default semi-transparent red. col_uninfected Color uninfected trajectories. Default semi-transparent blue. show_legend Logical; whether draw legend. Default TRUE. ... Additional graphical parameters passed plot().

title: Plot antibody trajectory with model fit — plot_trajectory
text: Invisible NULL. Called side effect producing plot.

title: Plot antibody trajectory with model fit — plot_trajectory
code: # \donttest{ fit <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                         n_iteration = 2000, burnin = 1000, thinning = 1) #> 1000 #> MCMC complete in 27 seconds. Use summary() to view estimates. plot_trajectory(fit, id = 1)  # }

title: Plot posterior waning curves — plot_waning
text: Shows fraction peak antibody remaining time since infection, posterior median 95% credible band waning parameter group. Matches style Figure 1D Tsang et al. (2022).

title: Plot posterior waning curves — plot_waning
code: plot_waning(fit, days = 400, cols = NULL, main = NULL, show_legend = TRUE, ...)

title: Plot posterior waning curves — plot_waning
text: fit seroreconstruct_fit seroreconstruct_joint object. single-season fits currently supported. days Maximum number days plot x-axis. Default 400. cols Optional character vector colors, one per group. main Optional plot title. show_legend Logical; whether draw legend. Default TRUE. ... Additional graphical parameters passed plot().

title: Plot posterior waning curves — plot_waning
text: Invisible NULL. Called side effect producing plot.

title: Plot posterior waning curves — plot_waning
code: # \donttest{ fit <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                         n_iteration = 2000, burnin = 1000, thinning = 1) #> 1000 #> MCMC complete in 27 seconds. Use summary() to view estimates. plot_waning(fit)  # }

what: Run the MCMC for the Bayesian model — sero_reconstruct
title: Run the MCMC for the Bayesian model — sero_reconstruct
text: main function run MCMC Bayesian model, obtain individual dynamics, model parameters infection probability, boosting, waning, measurement error.

title: Run the MCMC for the Bayesian model — sero_reconstruct
code: sero_reconstruct(   inputdata,   inputILI,   n_iteration = 2000,   burnin = 1000,   thinning = 1,   group_by = NULL,   shared = NULL,   subject_ids = NULL )

title: Run the MCMC for the Bayesian model — sero_reconstruct
text: inputdata data running MCMC, dataframe format. format data package. includes: 1) age_group (0: children, 1: adults, 2: older adults), 2) start_time: start follow-, 3) end_time: end follow-, 4) time1: date first serum collection, 5) time2: date second serum collection, 6) time3: date third serum collection, 7) HAI_titer_1: HAI titer first serum collection, 8) HAI_titer_2: HAI titer second serum collection, 9) HAI_titer_3: HAI titer third serum collection. inputILI data influenza activity used inference. row number match date inputdata. n_iteration number iterations MCMC. burnin iteration burn-MCMC. thinning number thinning MCMC. group_by Optional formula specifying grouping variables (e.g., ~age_group). provided, independent MCMCs fit combination grouping variables. formula uses interaction semantics: ~age + vac means age--vac combinations. Returns seroreconstruct_multi object. shared Optional character vector specifying parameters share across groups group_by also provided. Measurement error parameters always shared (lab assay property, identical across groups). Valid values: "error" (measurement error , default shared non-NULL), "boosting_waning" (also share antibody boosting waning across groups). specified, single joint MCMC run groups pooled together, sharing specified parameters estimating group-specific infection probabilities. Returns seroreconstruct_joint object. subject_ids Optional vector (character, numeric, factor) subject identifiers, one per row inputdata. provided, stored fit object used plot_trajectory() look individuals ID rather row index. Example: subject_ids = inputdata$household_id.

title: Run the MCMC for the Bayesian model — sero_reconstruct
text: seroreconstruct_fit object (group_by NULL)   seroreconstruct_multi object (group_by provided).   Use summary() extract model estimates.

title: Run the MCMC for the Bayesian model — sero_reconstruct
text: Multi-season support: inputdata contains optional integer column named season (0-indexed, contiguous 0 n_seasons - 1), model fits season-specific infection risk HAI protection parameters. season column present, individuals assigned single season (n_seasons = 1) behavior identical previous versions. Validated simulation recovery studies 7 seasons. Shared parameters: shared provided together group_by, single joint MCMC chain run individuals pooled. Measurement error boosting/waning parameters shared across groups (informed data), infection risk HAI protection parameters remain group-specific. statistically efficient independent chains groups share biological measurement properties. Single-group design: using group_by without shared, independent MCMCs fit group. compare children vs adults, fit group separately using group_by = ~age_group. Current limitation: summary() yet implemented fits n_seasons > 1. Multi-season posterior samples accessible directly fit object (e.g., fit$posterior_model_parameter).

title: Run the MCMC for the Bayesian model — sero_reconstruct
code: # \donttest{ a1 <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                         n_iteration = 2000, burnin = 1000, thinning = 1) #> 1000 #> MCMC complete in 26 seconds. Use summary() to view estimates. summary(a1) #>                                                                                                        Variable #> children (Waning) #>                                                                Fold-increase after 1 year for adults (Boosting) #>                                                                  Fold-decrease after 1 year for adults (Waning) #>                                                                              Infection probability for children #> pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10 #>                                         Infection probability for older adults with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10 #> children with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10 (Ref: Adults with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10) #>  Relative risk for older adults with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10 (Ref: Adults with pre-epidemic HAI titer < 10) #>  Point estimate Lower bound Upper bound #>            2.51        1.55        3.21 #>            3.09        3.74        2.41 #>            8.29        5.51       11.25 #>            1.07        1.05        1.08 #>            6.63        5.44        8.06 #>            1.11        1.07        1.20 #>            0.19        0.15        0.22 #>            0.19        0.16        0.23 #>            0.13        0.09        0.18 #>            0.42        0.34        0.51 #>            0.28        0.24        0.33 #>            0.19        0.13        0.26 #>            0.97        0.76        1.20 #>            1.51        1.19        1.89 #>            0.67        0.44        0.95 #>            0.68        0.45        0.96 # }

what: seroreconstruct: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection — seroreconstruct-package
title: seroreconstruct: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection — seroreconstruct-package
text: Bayesian framework inferring influenza infection status serial antibody measurements. Jointly estimates season-specific infection probabilities, antibody boosting waning infection, baseline HAI titer distributions via MCMC. Supports multi-season analysis subgroup comparisons via group_by interface. See Tsang et al. (2022) doi:10.1038/s41467-022-29310-8  methodological details.

title: seroreconstruct: Reconstructing Antibody Dynamics to Estimate the Risk of Influenza Virus Infection — seroreconstruct-package
text: Maintainer: Tim Tsang timkltsang@gmail.com (ORCID)

what: Simulation of the dataset of the Bayesian model — simulate_data
title: Simulation of the dataset of the Bayesian model — simulate_data
text: function simulate dataset, validation purpose.

title: Simulation of the dataset of the Bayesian model — simulate_data
code: simulate_data(inputdata, inputILI, para1, para2, n_groups = 3L)

title: Simulation of the dataset of the Bayesian model — simulate_data
text: inputdata data format running MCMC, dataframe format. inputILI data influenza activity used inference. row number match date inputdata. para1 Numeric vector active model parameters. Length depends number seasons S (determined season column inputdata, default S = 1): Elements 1–6 (shared): 1) random measurement error, 2) 2-fold error,     3) boosting children (log2), 4) waning children (log2),     5) boosting adults (log2), 6) waning adults (log2). Elements 7 6 + 3*S (per-season): infection risk scale     parameters children, adults, older adults, repeated season. Elements 6 + 3*S + 1 6 + 4*S (per-season): log risk     ratio 2-fold increase baseline HAI titer, one per season. Total length: 6 + (G + 1)*S G n_groups (e.g., 10 G=3 S=1, 34 G=3 S=7). See para1 example G = 3, S = 1. para2 Numeric vector baseline HAI titer distributions. Length 20 * S: season, 10 probabilities children (HAI titer levels 0–9) followed 10 probabilities adults. See para2 example S = 1. n_groups Number groups infection risk parameters (default 3 standard 3-age-group model).

title: Simulation of the dataset of the Bayesian model — simulate_data
text: simulated data based input parameter vectors, format equal input data.

title: Simulation of the dataset of the Bayesian model — simulate_data
code: simulated <- simulate_data(inputdata, flu_activity, para1, para2)

title: Per-individual infection estimates — table_infections
text: Summarizes posterior infection status, timing, baseline titer individual dataset.

title: Per-individual infection estimates — table_infections
code: table_infections(fit)

title: Per-individual infection estimates — table_infections
text: fit seroreconstruct_fit seroreconstruct_joint object.

title: Per-individual infection estimates — table_infections
text: data frame one row per individual columns:   Individual (row index), Infection_prob (posterior mean   probability infection), Infection_time_mean (mean infection time   among infected samples), Baseline_titer_mean (mean imputed baseline   HAI titer).

title: Per-individual infection estimates — table_infections
code: # \donttest{ fit <- sero_reconstruct(inputdata, flu_activity,                         n_iteration = 2000, burnin = 1000, thinning = 1) #> 1000 #> MCMC complete in 26 seconds. Use summary() to view estimates. head(table_infections(fit)) #>   Individual Infection_prob Infection_time_mean Baseline_titer_mean #> 1          1           0.14               948.1                0.44 #> 2          2           0.00                  NA                0.51 #> 3          3           0.00                  NA                0.47 #> 4          4           0.00                  NA                4.54 #> 5          5           0.00                  NA                0.54 #> 6          6           0.00                  NA                0.49 # }

title: Summary table of model parameters with credible intervals — table_parameters
text: Extracts posterior summaries (mean, median, credible intervals) active model parameters.

code: table_parameters(fit, probs = c(0.025, 0.975))

title: Summary table of model parameters with credible intervals — table_parameters
text: fit seroreconstruct_fit, seroreconstruct_joint, seroreconstruct_multi object. probs Numeric vector length 2 giving lower upper quantile probabilities credible interval. Default c(0.025, 0.975) 95% interval.

title: Summary table of model parameters with credible intervals — table_parameters
text: data frame columns: Parameter, Mean, Median, Lower, Upper.

title: Summary table of model parameters with credible intervals — table_parameters
seroreconstruct 1.1.2
text: Added LAPACK/BLAS linkage src/Makevars Linux/Debian, fixing undefined symbol: dpotrf_ installation failure CRAN’s Debian (clang-21) pre-test environment.

title: seroreconstruct 1.1.1
text: Fixed variable length array (VLA) warnings C++ code flagged GCC 14 Windows (-Wvla). Replaced std::vector<double>. Removed obsolete CXX_STD = CXX11 Makevars files SystemRequirements — default C++ standard sufficient.

title: seroreconstruct 1.1.0
text: sero_reconstruct() gains group_by argument subgroup analysis — fit independent MCMCs age groups, vaccination status, strata defined one-sided formula (e.g., ~age_group). sero_reconstruct() gains shared argument joint models — run single MCMC shares measurement error ("error") /antibody boosting/waning ("boosting_waning") across groups estimating group-specific infection risk. Measurement error always shared comparing groups virus subtype. sero_reconstruct() gains subject_ids argument ID-based individual lookup plot_trajectory(). Multi-season support — add 0-indexed integer season column input data; model estimates season-specific infection risk HAI protection parameters. S3 classes seroreconstruct_fit, seroreconstruct_joint, seroreconstruct_multi print() summary() methods. New plot functions: plot_trajectory(), plot_boosting(), plot_waning(), plot_infection_prob(), plot_diagnostics(). New table functions: table_parameters(), table_infections(). simulate_data() — generate synthetic HAI titer datasets validation power analysis.

title: seroreconstruct 1.1.0
text: output_model_estimate() deprecated. Use table_parameters() table_infections() instead.

title: seroreconstruct 1.0.0
text: Initial release. Core Bayesian MCMC framework inferring influenza infection status, antibody dynamics, individual infection risks longitudinal HAI titer data. Based Tsang TK et al. (2022) Nat Commun 13:1557. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-29310-8
